summary: Gruber compares BBEdit 7's new built-in Sort Lines and Process Duplicate Lines plug-ins with Craig Maynard's newly revived LineSort plug-in, which has been released as free and open-source software. He explains how the shift from C plug-ins to Perl filters and AppleScripts reduced the BBEdit plug-in ecosystem, and how LineSort's absence on Mac OS X was keenly felt. He analyzes the UI tradeoffs between Bare Bones's modal dialogs (keyboard-driven) and LineSort's floating palette (mouse-driven), and compares their AppleScript interfaces. He concludes the situation is a win for everyone, since LineSort is now free and works across Mac OS versions.
key_points: The BBEdit plug-in ecosystem declined not from lack of interest but because Perl filters and AppleScripts became easier alternatives to writing C plug-ins.
Modal dialogs have underappreciated advantages over floating palettes, particularly keyboard-driven shortcuts that don't conflict with menu commands.
LineSort's UI is less polished but more straightforward, combining sort and duplicate removal in a single action versus BBEdit's two separate dialogs.
BBEdit's AppleScript syntax for sorting is more verbose but more consistent with the application's overall scripting model.
The competition between free LineSort and BBEdit 7's built-in tools is a genuine win-win, with no revenue conflict since LineSort went freeware.
money_quotes: I don't think the relative dearth of recent plug-ins is because there is any less developer interest in BBEdit — I think it's simply that Perl filters and AppleScripts have become so much easier to write.
Modal dialogs get a bad rap from many users, so you might find it curious that Bare Bones would choose to use them for new plug-ins.

These shortcuts don't conflict with normal menu command shortcuts, because most menu commands are disabled when a modal dialog is open. A floating palette such as LineSort's can only be driven using the mouse.
I definitely wouldn't describe LineSort's interface as pretty, but it seems more straightforward, mainly because it doesn't offer as many checkboxes as do Bare Bones's plug-in dialogs.
There's no reason not to declare the situation a win for everyone.
key_insight: The decline of BBEdit's C plug-in ecosystem wasn't a loss but a natural evolution, as scripting languages made text manipulation tasks easier — and when both built-in tools and free third-party alternatives coexist, users win.
